content.text: Design the control logic for a bank of elevators: passengers make hall calls (UP/DOWN on a floor) and car calls (a destination inside a car); the system dispatches a suitable car and each car moves efficiently using the SCAN (elevator) algorithm without starving floors. Model each car as a state machine; dispatch policy is pluggable. Reject invalid floors/directions/unknown cars/out-of-service cars. Thread-safe under concurrent button presses; pressing the same floor twice is one stop.

# Design an Elevator System

> **Problem statement (verbatim):** Design the control logic for a bank of elevators: passengers make hall calls (UP/DOWN on a floor) and car calls (a destination inside a car); the system dispatches a suitable car and each car moves efficiently using the SCAN (elevator) algorithm without starving floors. Model each car as a state machine; dispatch policy is pluggable. Reject invalid floors/directions/unknown cars/out-of-service cars. Thread-safe under concurrent button presses; pressing the same floor twice is one stop (idempotent).

---

## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate the bank-of-elevators problem, ask clarifying questions (hall vs car calls, SCAN, pluggable dispatch). Don't write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — `request_elevator`, `select_floor`, `tick`, `set_maintenance`, `pending_stops`. "Before classes, here's the contract external code calls." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — 4 boxes: `ElevatorControlSystem`, `ElevatorCar` (state machine), `DispatchStrategy` + 2 concretes, `StopEvent`. Mention enums verbally. | ✍️ DRAW |
| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — State (car), Strategy (dispatch), Template Method (`_lock`). Then: "I see a check-then-orient race in `request_stop` — I'll fix it with a per-car lock in §9." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — the key insight: car = self-guarding state machine + SCAN + stop-set (idempotent). | 🗣️ SAY |
| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (§6): `Direction`/`CarState` enums → `ElevatorCar` state machine + SCAN → `DispatchStrategy` + nearest → `ElevatorControlSystem` skeleton → `request_elevator()` → `tick()`. | ✍️ WRITE |
| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 2** — dispatch + SCAN order, then idempotent double-press. Run `main.py` (Uber coding env). | 🗣️ SAY / run |
| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-car lock via the no-op `_lock` hook; `ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem` overrides one method. Write subclass only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
| 55–60 min | **Follow-up questions** — hardest follow-ups from §9 (distributed dispatch, 10M buildings, sweep). | ❓ IF ASKED |

**Two interview modes — pick one at the top:**
- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output. Demo cases verbally with state-transition tables. Write code, don't execute.
- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Write and RUN `main.py` in §7. Show actual terminal output. This write-up is Mode B — the run is proven below.

**The one thing that makes interviewers flag you "strong hire":** Right after the diagram, proactively say "I see a race condition in how a car queues a stop and picks its direction — `_orient_from_idle` iterates the stop set with `min`, and a concurrent press adding to that set mid-iteration raises `Set changed size during iteration`. I'll keep the core clean and show the per-car-lock fix at the end." Say it before you're asked.

---

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)

> 💬 "We're building the brain that runs a bank of elevators in a tall building. People standing on a floor press UP or DOWN — that's a 'hall call'. People already inside a car press a destination floor — that's a 'car call'. The system has to pick which car answers each hall call, and each car has to move smartly so nobody waits forever. The classic way a car moves is the SCAN algorithm: keep going one direction serving every stop on the way, and only turn around when there's nothing left ahead. I'm building the control logic, not the motors or the doors hardware."

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)

**You:** "I'll model this as a *simulation* driven by a `tick()` call — each tick every car makes one SCAN move — rather than real wall-clock motion. That keeps it deterministic and testable. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep, that's exactly what we want for an interview."
*✓ scoped: time advances via `tick()`, no real timers — fully deterministic.*

**You:** "Core operations are: a hall call (`request_elevator(floor, direction)`), a car call (`select_floor(car_id, floor)`), advancing time (`tick`), and an admin maintenance toggle. Is that the full v1 surface?"
**Interviewer:** "That's the set. Maintenance is a nice touch — keep it."
*✓ four public operations; maintenance is in scope as a first-class mode.*

**You:** "For the movement algorithm I'll use SCAN — the elevator goes one way until there are no more requests that way, then reverses. That's how real elevators and disk-head schedulers avoid starving the far end. Good?"
**Interviewer:** "SCAN is what I'm looking for. Make sure no floor starves."
*✓ SCAN named explicitly; anti-starvation is a hard requirement.*

**You:** "Dispatch policy — which car answers a hall call — I'll make *pluggable* behind a strategy. Default is nearest-car; I'll also show a least-busy one. Reasonable?"
**Interviewer:** "Yes, pluggable dispatch is a big part of why this problem is interesting."
*✓ Strategy seam for dispatch; nearest-car default, least-busy alternative.*

**You:** "Pressing the same floor twice should be one stop — idempotent. I'll back the stop queue with a set so duplicates collapse automatically. And under concurrent presses I'll make it thread-safe with a per-car lock. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Perfect — that idempotency + thread-safety combo is exactly the edge I'd probe."
*✓ stop queue is a set (idempotent); thread-safety via per-car lock.*

**You:** "I'll reject bad inputs with typed errors: a floor out of range, a nonsensical direction like UP-from-top, an unknown car id, and a car call to a car that's out of service. Each is its own exception. Agreed?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed. Clear typed rejections are what I want to see."
*✓ five typed failure modes, all under one domain base.*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**

1. **Passenger makes a hall call** via `request_elevator(floor, direction)`
   - System validates the floor is in range and the direction is sensible (no UP from top, no DOWN from bottom, not IDLE)
   - Dispatch strategy picks one in-service car; the floor is queued on it; returns the chosen car id
   - If no in-service car exists, reject with `NoCarAvailableError`
2. **Passenger makes a car call** via `select_floor(car_id, floor)`
   - Forces the destination onto THAT car (no re-dispatch — they already boarded)
   - Rejects unknown car id, out-of-range floor, and out-of-service car
3. **Each car moves by SCAN** — keep going one direction serving stops in order, turn around only when nothing remains ahead; every requested floor is served exactly once and no floor starves
4. **Pressing the same floor twice is one stop** (idempotent) — the stop queue is a set
5. **Time advances via `tick()`** — every car makes one SCAN move per tick and returns the stops that happened
6. **Admin can take a car in/out of service** via `set_maintenance(car_id, on)` — out-of-service cars are skipped by dispatch and reject car calls
7. **Thread-safe under concurrent button presses** — concurrent presses on the SAME car serialise; presses on DIFFERENT cars run in parallel; no lost or duplicated stop

**Out of scope**
- Door/motor hardware control (we model state, not physics — out of system boundary)
- Real wall-clock timing / acceleration curves (we tick discretely — downstream concern)
- Passenger weight limits / overload sensors (a separate safety subsystem)
- Emergency / fire-recall modes (security feature, scoped out for v1 — would be a new `CarState`)

> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK, before I model anything I want to lock down that this is a tick-driven simulation with SCAN movement and pluggable dispatch — that's the whole shape. With that, let me walk through the actors and flows."

---

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**
- **Hall passenger** — standing on a floor, presses UP or DOWN; triggers `request_elevator`.
- **Car passenger** — inside a car, presses a destination; triggers `select_floor`.
- **Simulation clock / driver** — calls `tick()` to advance every car one SCAN step (in production, a real timer thread).
- **Building admin** — takes a car in/out of service via `set_maintenance`.

**Core flows**
1. Hall passenger: press UP on floor 3 → strategy picks nearest car → floor queued on that car → car id returned
2. Car passenger: inside car-A, press 8 → floor 8 queued on car-A (forced, no dispatch)
3. Driver: `tick()` → every car makes one SCAN move → stops that opened doors this tick returned as `StopEvent`s
4. Admin: `set_maintenance(car-A, on=True)` → car-A drops its queue, goes MAINTENANCE, vanishes from dispatch
5. Idempotency: press floor 7 three times on car-A → queued exactly once → served exactly once

---

## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)

> **ElevatorControlSystem (the bank)** — The thing external code talks to: it owns all the cars, validates calls, and runs dispatch. Owns system-wide state (the car registry, the dispatch policy) and coordinates everything. *Accepted — this is the orchestrator.*
>
> **ElevatorCar** — A real thing with a current floor, a state (parked / moving / doors open / out of service), a direction, and its own set of pending stops. It has both data AND behavior — it runs its own SCAN move and guards its own state transitions. *Accepted — the state-machine entity, the heart of the design.*
>
> **DispatchStrategy** — The rule for "which car answers this hall call". It's pure policy with no state of its own, and the business will want to swap it (nearest today, least-busy tomorrow). That's a behavior worth its own abstraction. *Accepted — a Strategy interface with concrete implementations.*
>
> **StopEvent** — When a car opens its doors at a floor, we emit a small immutable record (which car, which floor, which direction). It has its own meaning as "a stop that happened" and lets callers verify SCAN order without poking car internals. *Accepted — a frozen value object.*
>
> **HallCall / CarCall** — Tempting to model a call as its own object. But a hall call is fully captured by `(floor, direction)` and a car call by `(car_id, floor)` — once dispatched, the only thing that survives is a floor in a car's stop set. A standalone Call class would just be a bag we throw away after queueing. *Rejected — modeled as method arguments; the surviving state is a floor in the car's set.*
>
> **Floor** — A real physical thing, but in the control logic it's just an integer in `[min_floor, max_floor]`. No behavior, no state of its own. *Rejected — modeled as an `int` with range validation.*
>
> **Direction** — A finite set: UP, DOWN, IDLE. No behavior. *Rejected as entity — modeled as an enum (shared vocabulary).*
>
> **CarState** — The four modes a car can be in. Finite, no behavior. *Rejected as entity — modeled as an enum that the `ElevatorCar` state machine guards transitions over.*
>
> **Passenger / Rider** — An actor, not stored state. They CALL the system; the system never holds a Passenger object. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.*
>
> **Building** — The container. Its only relevant attributes (`min_floor`, `max_floor`) are config passed into the system. *Rejected — modeled as two ints on the orchestrator and each car.*

### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ElevatorControlSystem** | Validate every public call and dispatch hall calls to a car | `_cars: dict[str, ElevatorCar]` (id → car), `_min_floor: int`, `_max_floor: int`, `_strategy: DispatchStrategy` (the pluggable policy) | Every public call reaches a defined outcome — queued on a valid in-service car, or rejected with a typed error |
| **ElevatorCar** | Own its physical state and pending stops; make one SCAN move per tick | `car_id: str` (**id**), `current_floor: int`, `state: CarState`, `direction: Direction`, `_stops: set[int]` (idempotent queue) | Only legal state transitions; a stop is served exactly once; same floor twice = one stop |
| **DispatchStrategy** | Pick ONE car for a hall call (or none) | *(interface — no state)* | Returns a car from the candidates given, or `None` — never invents a car |
| **StopEvent** | Immutable record of a door-open at a floor | `car_id: str`, `floor: int`, `direction: Direction` (frozen) | History never changes once recorded |

**Enums** (shared vocabulary, not entities):
- `Direction` — `UP`, `DOWN`, `IDLE`
- `CarState` — `IDLE`, `MOVING`, `DOORS_OPEN`, `MAINTENANCE`

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro

> 🎙️ "So the model has four objects. `ElevatorControlSystem` is the brain — it owns the cars by id and a dispatch strategy, and it's the only thing that validates inputs and routes hall calls. `ElevatorCar` is the interesting one: it's a state machine that owns its own pending stops as a set, and it runs its own SCAN move — the brain never reaches in and sets a car's state directly. `DispatchStrategy` is the seam — the brain asks it which car wins, so we can swap nearest-car for least-busy without touching dispatch. `StopEvent` is just a frozen receipt of a stop that happened. The pieces I deliberately left OUT are HallCall, CarCall, Floor, and Passenger — a call is just method arguments that collapse to a floor in a car's set, a floor is an int, and passengers are actors that call us, not state we store."

---

## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)

| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `request_elevator` | `(floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str` | id of the assigned car | `InvalidFloorError`, `InvalidDirectionError`, `NoCarAvailableError` | A passenger on a floor presses UP/DOWN; the strategy picks a car and queues the floor |
| `select_floor` | `(car_id: str, floor: int) -> None` | — | `UnknownCarError`, `InvalidFloorError`, `CarOutOfServiceError` | A passenger inside a car picks a destination; forced onto that car |
| `tick` | `() -> list[StopEvent]` | the stops that opened doors this tick | — | Advance the simulation one step — every car makes one SCAN move |
| `set_maintenance` | `(car_id: str, on: bool) -> None` | — | `UnknownCarError` | Take a car in/out of service |
| `pending_stops` | `(car_id: str) -> frozenset[int]` | read-only snapshot of a car's queue | `UnknownCarError` | Inspect a car's stop queue (used to prove idempotency) |

> 🎙️ "So the whole system is five public calls — two that take requests in (`request_elevator`, `select_floor`), one that drives time forward (`tick`), one admin toggle, and one read-only inspector. Everything else is internal: the cars move themselves by SCAN, the strategy picks the car. If I were handed this interface cold, I'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a single class."

---

## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)

### 4.1 Per-class derivation

#### 4.1.1 ElevatorControlSystem (the orchestrator)

**(a) One-line role** — The system's public API: external code calls it to make hall/car calls, advance time, and toggle maintenance. Every operation flows through here.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "dispatches a suitable car" / "unknown cars rejected" | A registry of cars keyed by id |
| "reject invalid floors" | The building's floor range |
| "dispatch policy is pluggable" | A reference to the current `DispatchStrategy` |

```
class ElevatorControlSystem:
    - _cars: dict[str, ElevatorCar]
    - _min_floor: int
    - _max_floor: int
    - _strategy: DispatchStrategy
```

*State placement trade-off:* A car's pending stops, current floor, and state are **physical** to the car — they describe the car's condition — so they live on `ElevatorCar`, not the orchestrator. The orchestrator only holds **relational/system** state: the registry and the policy. That keeps the orchestrator from duplicating per-car bookkeeping.

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "passengers make hall calls" | `request_elevator(floor, direction) -> str` |
| "car calls (a destination inside a car)" | `select_floor(car_id, floor) -> None` |
| "each car moves … SCAN" (advance time) | `tick() -> list[StopEvent]` |
| "out-of-service cars" | `set_maintenance(car_id, on) -> None` |
| "pressing the same floor twice is one stop" (inspect) | `pending_stops(car_id) -> frozenset[int]` |
| concurrency hook (Template Method) | `_lock(car) -> contextmanager` |

```
class ElevatorControlSystem:
    - _cars: dict[str, ElevatorCar]
    - _min_floor: int
    - _max_floor: int
    - _strategy: DispatchStrategy

    + ElevatorControlSystem(cars, min_floor, max_floor, strategy=None)
    + request_elevator(floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str
    + select_floor(car_id: str, floor: int) -> None
    + tick() -> list[StopEvent]
    + set_maintenance(car_id: str, on: bool) -> None
    + pending_stops(car_id: str) -> frozenset[int]
    # _lock(car) -> ContextManager   (no-op hook; thread-safe subclass overrides)
    # _require_car(car_id) -> ElevatorCar
    # _validate_floor(floor) -> None
    # _validate_hall_call(floor, direction) -> None
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does `request_elevator` return the car id?** So the passenger UI can light up "car A is coming". The actual stop lives inside the car; the id is the only thing the caller needs.
- **Why does `select_floor` force the car (no dispatch)?** Because the passenger already boarded — you can't re-route them to a different car. Dispatch only applies to hall calls.
- **Why a `_lock(car)` no-op hook?** The clean core has zero lock code. The thread-safe subclass overrides only this one method. The diff is ~10 lines.

#### 4.1.2 ElevatorCar (the state machine)

**(a) One-line role** — The only class allowed to change a car's state and the only owner of that car's pending stops; it runs its own SCAN move.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "each car … current floor" | `current_floor: int` |
| "model each car as a state machine" | `state: CarState`, `direction: Direction` |
| "SCAN without starving floors" + "same floor twice is one stop" | `_stops: set[int]` (idempotent queue) |
| "reject invalid floors" | `min_floor`, `max_floor` |

```
class ElevatorCar:
    - car_id: str
    - min_floor: int
    - max_floor: int
    - current_floor: int
    - state: CarState
    - direction: Direction
    - _stops: set[int]
```

*State placement trade-off:* `_stops` is a **set**, not a list or a heap. SCAN doesn't need insertion order — it needs "is there a stop above/below me?", which a set answers, and the set gives idempotency for free. A list would let duplicates in; a heap would impose an order SCAN must override on every turnaround.

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| queue a destination (idempotent) | `request_stop(floor) -> bool` (True if newly added) |
| advance one SCAN step | `step() -> StopEvent \| None` |
| take in/out of service | `set_maintenance(on) -> None` |
| "dispatch a suitable car" needs to know availability/load | `in_service() -> bool`, `is_busy() -> bool`, `pending_stops() -> frozenset[int]` |

```
class ElevatorCar:
    - car_id: str
    - min_floor: int
    - max_floor: int
    - current_floor: int = 0
    - state: CarState = IDLE
    - direction: Direction = IDLE
    - _stops: set[int]

    + ElevatorCar(car_id, min_floor, max_floor, current_floor=0)
    + request_stop(floor: int) -> bool
    + step() -> StopEvent | None
    + set_maintenance(on: bool) -> None
    + in_service() -> bool
    + is_busy() -> bool
    + pending_stops() -> frozenset[int]
    # _orient_from_idle(), _open_doors_here(), _advance_one_floor(),
    # _reorient_after_stop(), _stops_in_current_direction(), _flip_direction(), _park()
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does `request_stop` return a bool?** It returns True only if the floor was *newly* added. That boolean IS the idempotency signal — a duplicate press returns False and changes nothing.
- **Why does `set_maintenance(True)` clear the stops?** A car physically can't serve queued floors while out of service, so we drop them rather than strand them. Coming back resets to IDLE so the next call re-orients cleanly.
- **Why does only `step()` move `current_floor`?** Single source of truth for motion. The orchestrator never moves a car; it only ticks it.

#### 4.1.3 DispatchStrategy (the Strategy interface)

**(a) One-line role** — The single seam behind which the "which car answers this hall call" policy lives.

**(b) State derivation** — A strategy is pure policy; it holds no state. (No state block beyond the interface.)

```
interface DispatchStrategy:
    (no fields — stateless policy)
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this interface |
|---|---|
| "dispatches a suitable car" / "dispatch policy is pluggable" | `select(cars, floor, direction) -> ElevatorCar \| None` |

```
interface DispatchStrategy:
    + select(cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None

class NearestCarStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
    + select(cars, floor, direction) -> ElevatorCar | None   # closest car wins

class LeastBusyStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
    + select(cars, floor, direction) -> ElevatorCar | None   # fewest pending stops wins
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does `select` get a pre-filtered list?** The orchestrator filters to in-service cars first, so a strategy only decides WHICH available car wins — it never has to know about maintenance.
- **Why can it return `None`?** If the candidate list is empty (all cars out of service), the strategy says "none" and the orchestrator turns that into `NoCarAvailableError`. No silent drop.

#### 4.1.4 StopEvent (the value object)

**(a) One-line role** — An immutable receipt of a door-open: which car stopped, at which floor, heading which way.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "each car moves … using SCAN" (observable proof of order) | `car_id`, `floor`, `direction` |

```
class StopEvent (frozen):
    - car_id: str
    - floor: int
    - direction: Direction
```

**(c) Operations derivation** — A value object has no behavior; it's read, never mutated.

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| (none — pure data record) | *(no methods; frozen dataclass)* |

```
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class StopEvent:
    - car_id: str
    - floor: int
    - direction: Direction
    (no methods — frozen value record)
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why frozen?** A stop that already happened is history — nothing should edit it. `frozen=True` makes Python enforce that.
- **Why return a list of these from `tick`?** So a caller (or test) can verify SCAN order externally without reaching into the car's private `_stops`.

### 4.2 Final consolidated class diagram

🗣️ **SAY (don't draw):** "`StopEvent` is just a frozen 3-field data holder — nothing interesting; I'll mention it but keep the diagram on the classes with real logic." (I draw it small below anyway since it's load-bearing for verifying SCAN order.)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class ElevatorControlSystem {
        -dict cars
        -int min_floor
        -int max_floor
        -DispatchStrategy strategy
        +request_elevator(floor, direction) str
        +select_floor(car_id, floor) None
        +tick() list~StopEvent~
        +set_maintenance(car_id, on) None
        +pending_stops(car_id) frozenset~int~
        #_lock(car) ContextManager
    }
    class ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem {
        -Lock map_lock
        -dict car_locks
        #_lock(car) ContextManager
    }
    class ElevatorCar {
        +str car_id
        +int current_floor
        +CarState state
        +Direction direction
        -set stops
        +request_stop(floor) bool
        +step() StopEvent
        +set_maintenance(on) None
        +in_service() bool
        +is_busy() bool
        +pending_stops() frozenset~int~
    }
    class DispatchStrategy {
        <<interface>>
        +select(cars, floor, direction) ElevatorCar
    }
    class NearestCarStrategy {
        +select(cars, floor, direction) ElevatorCar
    }
    class LeastBusyStrategy {
        +select(cars, floor, direction) ElevatorCar
    }
    class StopEvent {
        +str car_id
        +int floor
        +Direction direction
    }
    ElevatorControlSystem o-- "1..*" ElevatorCar : owns by id
    ElevatorControlSystem --> "1" DispatchStrategy : asks
    ElevatorControlSystem ..> StopEvent : emits
    ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem --|> ElevatorControlSystem
    NearestCarStrategy ..|> DispatchStrategy
    LeastBusyStrategy ..|> DispatchStrategy
    ElevatorCar ..> StopEvent : creates on stop
```

**Relationships, one at a time:**
- **`ElevatorControlSystem` o-- `ElevatorCar` (aggregation, 1..\*):** the system holds a reference to a bank of cars by id. Open diamond because the cars are passed IN at construction — they could in principle outlive the controller. The reference is FOR dispatch and routing at runtime.
- **`ElevatorControlSystem` --> `DispatchStrategy` (association, 1):** the orchestrator holds one strategy and calls `select` on it. **This is the extensibility SEAM** — the orchestrator depends on the abstraction, never on a concrete rule.
- **`NearestCarStrategy` / `LeastBusyStrategy` ..|> `DispatchStrategy` (realization):** both concretes implement the one `select` method; the orchestrator can't tell them apart.
- **`ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem` --|> `ElevatorControlSystem` (inheritance):** the thread-safe version extends the core and overrides ONLY the `_lock` hook. Everything else is inherited unchanged.
- **`ElevatorCar` ..> `StopEvent` and `ElevatorControlSystem` ..> `StopEvent` (dependency):** the car creates a `StopEvent` when it opens its doors; the orchestrator collects and returns them. Neither owns the events long-term.

**Pattern Map**

| Class / Interface | Pattern | One-line reason |
|---|---|---|
| `ElevatorCar` (`state` + transition methods) | **State** | the car is in exactly one of four states and guards its own legal transitions |
| `DispatchStrategy` + concretes | **Strategy** | the orchestrator depends on the abstraction; new dispatch rules plug in as classes |
| `ElevatorControlSystem._lock` hook | **Template Method** | no-op in the clean core; `ThreadSafe…` subclass overrides the one hook |

> 🎙️ **Script — walk the diagram box-by-box:** "I put `ElevatorControlSystem` in the center because every operation flows through it. Hanging off it is `ElevatorCar` — the state machine that owns its floor, its state, and its stop set, and runs its own SCAN move. On the right is `DispatchStrategy` — the Strategy seam, an interface so the building can swap nearest-car for least-busy without touching dispatch; `NearestCarStrategy` and `LeastBusyStrategy` implement it. `StopEvent` is the frozen receipt a car emits when it opens its doors. And `ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem` sits below the orchestrator — it's the §9 version that overrides just the `_lock` hook. The key seam is `DispatchStrategy`: the orchestrator never knows which policy is wired in."

> ⚠️ **PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG:** "I notice there's a race condition in how a car queues a stop. `request_stop` adds to the stop set, and if the car was idle it orients by `min`-ing over that same set — which **iterates** it. If two passengers press buttons on the SAME car at the same instant, one thread's `set.add` can land while the other is mid-iteration in `_orient_from_idle`, and CPython raises `RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration`. (The set membership itself is GIL-atomic, so no stop is lost — the iteration-during-mutation error is the real defect.) I'll keep the core clean and single-threaded for now, and show you exactly how I'd fix it with a per-car lock in §9."

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)

> "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to the data it has. `ElevatorControlSystem` owns dispatch and validation because it's the only thing that sees ALL cars. `ElevatorCar` owns its own state transitions and SCAN movement because its floor, direction, and stop set are intrinsic to it — physical state belongs on the entity, not the orchestrator. `DispatchStrategy` owns the 'which car' decision because that's a pure policy with no state, and isolating it is what lets us swap it freely. The orchestrator never duplicates a car's movement logic, and a car never reaches out to peek at other cars."

### 5.2 Applied patterns

**State (the car's lifecycle)**
- **Why it fits HERE:** A car is in exactly one of IDLE / MOVING / DOORS_OPEN / MAINTENANCE, and only some transitions are legal (e.g. MAINTENANCE drops everything; a stop is only burned in DOORS_OPEN). Instead of `if car.state == "moving"` scattered across the orchestrator, the car guards its own transitions in `step()` and `set_maintenance()`.
- **Seam:** `ElevatorCar.state: CarState` + the transition methods (`request_stop`, `step`, `set_maintenance`) are the only writers of `state`.
- 🎙️ Script: "Each car is a state machine. The orchestrator never sets a car's state directly — it asks the car to move, and the car decides whether it parks, advances, or opens its doors. Illegal jumps are impossible by construction."
- 💬 Layman gloss: "It's like the car has its own little brain for whether it's parked, driving, or letting people in — the dispatcher just says 'take a step', it never reaches in and flips a switch."

**Strategy (pluggable dispatch)**
- **Why it fits HERE:** "Which car answers this hall call" is exactly the thing a building will want to tweak — nearest-car for low traffic, least-busy during rush hour, maybe zone-based later. So I extract `DispatchStrategy` and the orchestrator never hardcodes the policy.
- **Seam:** `DispatchStrategy.select(...) -> ElevatorCar | None`, with `NearestCarStrategy` and `LeastBusyStrategy`.
- 🎙️ Script: "Dispatch is behind a strategy interface. The orchestrator filters to in-service cars and asks the strategy to pick one. Swapping policy is a one-line constructor change."
- 💬 Layman gloss: "The dispatcher doesn't decide which elevator to send — it asks a rule-of-the-day. We can change the rule without rewiring the dispatcher."

**Template Method (the concurrency hook)**
- **Why it fits HERE:** The clean core must stay lock-free and readable. So the orchestrator calls a `_lock(car)` context manager that does nothing; the thread-safe subclass overrides only that hook with a real per-car lock.
- **Seam:** `ElevatorControlSystem._lock` (no-op) → `ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem._lock` (per-car RLock via the double-lock idiom).
- 🎙️ Script: "There's a placeholder method for locking. In the clean version it does nothing — zero lock code in that file. The thread-safe version is a subclass that replaces just that one method."
- 💬 Layman gloss: "There's an empty hook for locking. The safe version fills it in; the clean version leaves it empty. About ten lines change."

### 5.3 SOLID principles

- **Single Responsibility:** `ElevatorCar` owns physical state + SCAN, `DispatchStrategy` owns car selection, `ElevatorControlSystem` owns validation + coordination. Three reasons to change, three classes.
- **Open/Closed:** a new dispatch rule is a new `DispatchStrategy` subclass — zero core edits. A new car mode (e.g. fire-recall) is a new `CarState` value plus one guard.
- **Dependency Inversion:** `ElevatorControlSystem` depends on the `DispatchStrategy` abstraction, never on `NearestCarStrategy` directly — the concrete is injected.
- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem` is a drop-in for `ElevatorControlSystem` — same contract, only the `_lock` hook differs.

### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS

Every pattern above is in the §6 code: `CarState` + the car's transition methods (State), `DispatchStrategy` ABC with two concretes the orchestrator calls (Strategy), and the `_lock` no-op overridden by the subclass (Template Method). I did **not** claim Observer (no notification subscribers here — `StopEvent`s are returned synchronously, not pushed) or Factory (cars are constructed directly, no enum→object translation). Naming those would not survive `Cmd-F`.

## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach

### A. The Key Insight

> **Insight:** Name the movement algorithm — it's **SCAN**. The car goes one direction serving every stop on the way until nothing remains ahead, then turns around. Back the stop queue with a **set** so it's idempotent, and make each car a **self-guarding state machine** so the orchestrator never touches a car's internals.
>
> **Why it works:** SCAN is correct because the building is a 1-D line of floors and a car moving one direction can serve every request on that side in one sweep — no request is skipped and the far end is always reached within one turnaround, so nothing starves. The "which floor next" question is answered purely from the set ("is there a stop above me?"), so order doesn't need to be stored. And pressing a floor is naturally idempotent because adding to a set that already has the element is a no-op.
>
> **What breaks without it:** The naive version uses a FIFO queue of stops and serves them in press-order. Now the car yo-yos — floor 2, floor 9, floor 3, floor 8 — wasting trips, and a floor pressed twice gets served twice. Worse, if the orchestrator inlines `car.state = MOVING` and writes the direction itself, six months later someone adds a maintenance mode and forgets to clear the queue in one path — now a car "out of service" still tries to serve floors. By making `ElevatorCar` a set-backed state machine that runs SCAN itself, both bugs are impossible by construction.

### B. Per-Operation Algorithm

**`request_elevator(floor, direction)` — the dispatch pipeline**

*(1) Pseudocode:*
1. validate floor is in `[min_floor, max_floor]` → else `InvalidFloorError`
2. validate direction: not IDLE, not UP-from-top, not DOWN-from-bottom → else `InvalidDirectionError`
3. build candidate list = all cars where `in_service()`
4. `chosen = strategy.select(candidates, floor, direction)`
5. if `chosen is None` → `NoCarAvailableError`
6. under the per-car lock: `chosen.request_stop(floor)` (idempotent)
7. return `chosen.car_id`

*(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script — the pipeline method:* "This is the pipeline — validate, dispatch, queue. Step 2 is where I reject nonsense directions; step 4 is where Strategy pays off — the orchestrator hands the strategy the in-service cars and gets one back, never knowing if it's nearest or least-busy. Step 6 is the critical section: it's wrapped in the per-car lock so two presses on the same car serialise, and `request_stop` is idempotent so a double-press is one stop. The edge the interviewer probes — 'what if all cars are out of service?' — is step 5: `select` returns None and we raise `NoCarAvailableError`, never silently dropping the call."

*(3) DRY RUN — `request_elevator(floor=3, direction=UP)` with car-A at 0 (IDLE), car-B at 10 (IDLE), nearest-car strategy:*

| Step | Action | State change |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | validate floor 3 in [0,10] | ✓ |
| 2 | direction UP, not top floor | ✓ |
| 3 | candidates = [car-A, car-B] (both in service) | — |
| 4 | `strategy.select(...)` → distance A=3, B=7 | returns car-A |
| 6 | under lock: `car-A.request_stop(3)` | car-A `_stops={3}`, state IDLE→MOVING, direction IDLE→UP |
| 7 | return `"car-A"` | caller gets `"car-A"` |

> *Edge probe: what if a second passenger calls `request_elevator(3, UP)` while step 6 is mid-flight on car-A?* *Without a lock, one thread's `self._stops.add(floor)` can land while the other is inside `_orient_from_idle`'s `min(self._stops, ...)` iteration — raising `RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration`. (Set membership itself is GIL-atomic, so no stop is lost; the unhandled iteration error is the real defect.) §9 fixes it with a per-car RLock around step 6.*

**`select_floor(car_id, floor)` — the redemption/forced-queue path**

*(1) Pseudocode:*
1. `car = _require_car(car_id)` → else `UnknownCarError`
2. validate floor in range → else `InvalidFloorError`
3. if not `car.in_service()` → `CarOutOfServiceError`
4. under the per-car lock: `car.request_stop(floor)`

*(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script:* "`select_floor` is three guards and one queue. Unlike a hall call there's no dispatch — the passenger already boarded, so the floor is forced onto THAT car. Step 3 is the out-of-service guard the interviewer will probe; step 4 is the same idempotent, lock-guarded queue."

**`tick()` — advance every car one SCAN step**

*(1) Pseudocode:*
1. `events = []`
2. for each car: under its lock, `event = car.step()`; if event, append
3. return `events`

*(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script — the driver method:* "`tick` is the clock. Each car steps under its OWN lock, so different cars move in parallel and the same car never double-steps. `car.step()` is where SCAN lives — it parks if empty, opens doors if it's standing on a requested floor, otherwise moves one floor toward the current direction and re-checks."

**`ElevatorCar.step()` — one SCAN move (the algorithm core)**

*(1) Pseudocode:*
1. if MAINTENANCE or no stops → `_park()`, return None
2. if `current_floor in _stops` → `_open_doors_here()` (burn it, emit StopEvent, re-orient), return event
3. if no stops in current direction → `_flip_direction()`
4. move one floor toward direction (clamped to the shaft ends)
5. if now `current_floor in _stops` → `_open_doors_here()`, return event
6. return None

*(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script — the SCAN core:* "This is the heart. Step 1 parks an idle car. Step 2 handles the case where I'm already on a requested floor — burn it and open. Steps 3-4 are the SCAN rule: if there's nothing ahead the way I'm pointing, turn around, then take one step. Step 5 lets me open doors the same tick I arrive, so a stop is never skipped past."

**`set_maintenance` / `pending_stops`** — `set_maintenance` flips the car's state (clearing stops on the way in); `pending_stops` is an O(1) read-only snapshot. Both trace to requirements 6 and 4 respectively.

### C. Data Structure Choices

| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `dict[str, ElevatorCar]` | car registry | car calls and admin toggles are by id; a list would be O(n) per lookup and an unknown id wouldn't be a clean miss | O(1) get |
| `set[int]` on each car | pending stops | gives idempotency for free (double-press collapses) and answers "stop above/below me?" directly; a FIFO list yo-yos and allows duplicates; a heap imposes an order SCAN must override on every turnaround | O(1) add/discard, O(n) "any above" scan, n = floors |
| `frozenset[int]` snapshot | `pending_stops` return | hand callers a read-only view so they can't mutate the car's private set | O(n) to build, n small |
| linear scan of candidate cars | dispatch | a bank is ~2-8 cars; a fancy index buys nothing at this scale | O(c), c = cars (tiny) |

The "any stop above/below" scan is O(n) in floors, but n is the building height (tens), so it's a handful of comparisons per tick. *I'd switch the stop set to a sorted structure (two heaps, up-stops and down-stops) only if a single shaft served thousands of floors — never in a real building.*

### D. Tricky Logic

> **🧠 Claim:** Pressing the same floor twice must be exactly one stop (idempotent).
> **🔬 Proof:** With a FIFO list, two presses of floor 7 enqueue 7 twice; the car opens its doors at 7, serves it, and later opens AGAIN at 7 for the stale entry — a phantom stop.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `_stops` is a `set`; `request_stop` does `self._stops.add(floor)` and returns `floor not in self._stops` (checked before the add). The second press returns False and changes nothing. The 40-thread test in §7 hammers this exact case.

> **🧠 Claim:** A car must never skip past a requested floor.
> **🔬 Proof:** If `step()` moved a floor and returned without re-checking, a car heading up from 4 with a stop at 5 could move to 5 and then to 6 on the next tick without opening at 5.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `step()` re-checks `current_floor in self._stops` immediately after `_advance_one_floor()` and opens there the same tick (pseudocode step 5).

> **🧠 Claim:** The turnaround must be exact — turn only when nothing remains ahead.
> **🔬 Proof:** Turning too early strands the far end (starvation); turning too late runs the car into the shaft wall.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `_stops_in_current_direction()` asks "any stop strictly above (UP) / below (DOWN) me?" and `_advance_one_floor` flips only when that's False, clamping at `min_floor`/`max_floor`.

> **🧠 Claim:** Going into maintenance must not strand queued stops.
> **🔬 Proof:** If `set_maintenance(True)` left `_stops` populated, the car would resume those stops when it came back — serving requests passengers may have re-pressed on another car.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `set_maintenance(True)` calls `self._stops.clear()` and sets direction IDLE; coming back sets state IDLE so the next call re-orients.

> **🧠 Claim:** The compound `request_stop` (read set → add → orient if idle) must be atomic under contention — and the reason is NOT "last-writer-wins on direction".
> **🔬 Proof (the precise, reproducible defect):** `_orient_from_idle` runs `min(self._stops, key=...)`, which **iterates** the stop set. If another thread is concurrently doing `self._stops.add(floor)` (also on an IDLE car), the set changes size mid-iteration and CPython raises `RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration`. This is reproducible deterministically with stops on both sides of `current_floor` and a low `sys.setswitchinterval`. Note what is NOT the bug: `set.add` / `in` / `discard` are individually GIL-atomic, so no stop is ever lost or duplicated, and the worst direction outcome is a benign last-writer-wins that still points at a real pending stop. The genuine corruption is the unhandled `RuntimeError` thrown out of the iteration.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** the orchestrator wraps `request_stop` in `with self._lock(car)`; the thread-safe subclass makes that a per-car RLock, so an add can never land while `_orient_from_idle` is iterating. `test_lockfree_base_corrupts_under_same_car_race` runs the exact barrier scenario against the lock-free base and asserts it raises that `RuntimeError`; `test_threadsafe_clean_and_consistent_under_same_car_race` runs the same scenario through the locked subclass and asserts it stays clean — together they prove the lock is load-bearing, not the GIL.

### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example

> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
> ```
> def select_floor(self, car_id, floor):
>     car = self._cars[car_id]          # bare lookup — KeyError leaks
>     car._stops.append(floor)          # list + direct field poke
> ```
> **Why it's wrong:** Three failure modes. (1) `self._cars[car_id]` raises a raw `KeyError` instead of a typed `UnknownCarError`. (2) `append` to a list lets duplicates in — pressing 5 twice is two stops. (3) Reaching into `_stops` directly bypasses the state machine, so the car never re-orients and an out-of-service car still accepts the stop. The correct version goes through `_require_car`, calls `car.request_stop(floor)` (set-backed, idempotent, self-orienting), and guards `in_service()`.

### `ElevatorCar` — the state machine + SCAN (`models.py`)

Role: the only class allowed to change a car's state and the only owner of its pending stops.

```python copy
from dataclasses import dataclass, field


@dataclass
class ElevatorCar:
    car_id: str
    min_floor: int
    max_floor: int
    current_floor: int = 0
    state: CarState = CarState.IDLE
    direction: Direction = Direction.IDLE
    # WHY: a SET, not a list. Pressing floor 5 twice must be ONE stop.
    _stops: set[int] = field(default_factory=set)

    def in_service(self) -> bool:
        return self.state is not CarState.MAINTENANCE

    def is_busy(self) -> bool:
        return bool(self._stops)

    def pending_stops(self) -> frozenset[int]:
        return frozenset(self._stops)

    def request_stop(self, floor: int) -> bool:
        # WHY: the ONE entry point that adds a stop. Returns True only if newly
        #      added — a duplicate press returns False (idempotency signal).
        if not self.min_floor <= floor <= self.max_floor:
            raise InvalidFloorError(
                f"floor {floor} outside [{self.min_floor}, {self.max_floor}] for car {self.car_id}"
            )

        if self.state is CarState.MAINTENANCE:
            raise CarOutOfServiceError(f"car {self.car_id} is out of service")

        newly_added = floor not in self._stops
        self._stops.add(floor)

        # WHY: a parked car that just got its first stop must pick a direction.
        if newly_added and self.state is CarState.IDLE:
            self._orient_from_idle()

        return newly_added

    def set_maintenance(self, on: bool) -> None:
        # WHY: in -> drop all stops (can't serve them); out -> back to IDLE.
        if on:
            self.state = CarState.MAINTENANCE
            self.direction = Direction.IDLE
            self._stops.clear()
        elif self.state is CarState.MAINTENANCE:
            self.state = CarState.IDLE
            self.direction = Direction.IDLE

    def step(self) -> StopEvent | None:
        # WHY: advance exactly one SCAN tick. Only place current_floor/state move.
        if self.state is CarState.MAINTENANCE or not self._stops:
            self._park()
            return None

        if self.current_floor in self._stops:
            return self._open_doors_here()

        self._advance_one_floor()

        # WHY: we may have landed on a requested floor — open this same tick so
        #      a stop is never skipped past.
        if self.current_floor in self._stops:
            return self._open_doors_here()

        return None

    def _orient_from_idle(self) -> None:
        # ⚠️ THREADING: min() ITERATES self._stops — the exact spot the per-car lock
        #      protects. A concurrent request_stop doing self._stops.add(floor) mutates
        #      the set mid-iteration and raises "Set changed size during iteration".
        #      The car stays lock-free; the orchestrator serialises add-and-orient.
        target = min(self._stops, key=lambda f: abs(f - self.current_floor))
        self.direction = Direction.UP if target >= self.current_floor else Direction.DOWN
        self.state = CarState.MOVING

    def _open_doors_here(self) -> StopEvent:
        # WHY: burn the stop so it can't be served twice, open doors, record event.
        self._stops.discard(self.current_floor)
        self.state = CarState.DOORS_OPEN
        event = StopEvent(self.car_id, self.current_floor, self.direction)
        self._reorient_after_stop()
        return event

    def _advance_one_floor(self) -> None:
        # WHY: SCAN core — keep going while stops remain ahead, else flip.
        if not self._stops_in_current_direction():
            self._flip_direction()

        self.state = CarState.MOVING

        if self.direction is Direction.UP and self.current_floor < self.max_floor:
            self.current_floor += 1
        elif self.direction is Direction.DOWN and self.current_floor > self.min_floor:
            self.current_floor -= 1

    def _reorient_after_stop(self) -> None:
        if not self._stops:
            self.direction = Direction.IDLE
            return

        if not self._stops_in_current_direction():
            self._flip_direction()

    def _stops_in_current_direction(self) -> bool:
        if self.direction is Direction.UP:
            return any(f > self.current_floor for f in self._stops)

        if self.direction is Direction.DOWN:
            return any(f < self.current_floor for f in self._stops)

        return False

    def _flip_direction(self) -> None:
        if self.direction is Direction.UP:
            self.direction = Direction.DOWN
        elif self.direction is Direction.DOWN:
            self.direction = Direction.UP
        else:
            self._orient_from_idle()

    def _park(self) -> None:
        self.state = CarState.IDLE
        self.direction = Direction.IDLE
```

Reasoning: `request_stop` is the single mutation point — it validates, adds to the set, and orients an idle car. The `newly_added` boolean is the idempotency proof. Notice we re-check `current_floor in self._stops` AFTER moving in `step()` — that's what stops the car opening doors a tick late. The whole SCAN turnaround logic lives in three tiny helpers (`_stops_in_current_direction`, `_flip_direction`, `_reorient_after_stop`) so no single method gets deep.

⚠️ "What if a car is on a requested floor when it goes into maintenance?" → "`set_maintenance(True)` clears `_stops`, so there's nothing left to serve. The next call re-orients it from scratch."

### `DispatchStrategy` + concretes (`strategies.py`)

```python copy
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

from models import Direction, ElevatorCar


class DispatchStrategy(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def select(self, cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None:
        """Pick ONE car for a hall call, or None if none is suitable."""


class NearestCarStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
    def select(self, cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None:
        if not cars:
            return None

        # WHY: rank by (distance, busy?, id) — closest wins; idle beats busy; id breaks ties.
        return min(cars, key=lambda c: (abs(c.current_floor - floor), c.is_busy(), c.car_id))


class LeastBusyStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
    def select(self, cars: list[ElevatorCar], floor: int, direction: Direction) -> ElevatorCar | None:
        if not cars:
            return None

        # WHY: rank by (queue length, distance, id) — fewest stops wins.
        return min(cars, key=lambda c: (len(c.pending_stops()), abs(c.current_floor - floor), c.car_id))
```

🎙️ "Two policies, one interface. Both rank with a tuple key and `min`, so ties break deterministically — same inputs always pick the same car, which makes tests trivial."

#### `request_elevator` — the dispatch pipeline

Core logic: validate → build in-service candidates → strategy picks → queue under lock → return id.
Edge cases: bad floor, bad direction, all cars out of service.

```python copy
    def request_elevator(self, floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str:
        self._validate_hall_call(floor, direction)

        candidates = [car for car in self._cars.values() if car.in_service()]
        chosen = self._strategy.select(candidates, floor, direction)

        if chosen is None:
            raise NoCarAvailableError(f"no in-service car for hall call at floor {floor}")

        # WHY: hold the per-car lock only around the stop mutation, so two presses
        #      racing on the SAME car serialise and the floor is queued exactly once.
        with self._lock(chosen):
            chosen.request_stop(floor)

        return chosen.car_id
```

Reasoning: this is the pipeline — `_validate_hall_call` rejects nonsense before any state changes, the strategy fires on the in-service subset (the orchestrator never sees policy), and the only mutation (`request_stop`) sits inside the per-car lock. Notice we filter out-of-service cars BEFORE the strategy, so a strategy never has to think about maintenance. The `None` path is explicit — an unanswerable call raises, never returns silently.

🎙️ "Validate, dispatch through the strategy, queue under the lock, return the car id."
⚠️ "What if two people press the same hall button at once?" → "Both may route to the same car, but `request_stop` is idempotent — the floor's queued once. Under threads the per-car lock makes the queue-and-orient one atomic step."

    def tick(self) -> list[StopEvent]:
        events: list[StopEvent] = []

        for car in self._cars.values():

            with self._lock(car):
                event = car.step()

            if event is not None:
                events.append(event)

        return events
```

Reasoning: each car steps under its OWN lock, so different cars advance independently. `car.step()` carries all the SCAN logic; the orchestrator just collects the door-opens. The blank line before the `if` keeps the loop body unambiguous.

    def _require_car(self, car_id: str) -> ElevatorCar:
        # WHY: one place that turns an unknown id into a typed error, not a raw KeyError.
        car = self._cars.get(car_id)

        if car is None:
            raise UnknownCarError(f"no car with id {car_id!r}")

        return car

    def _validate_floor(self, floor: int) -> None:
        if not self._min_floor <= floor <= self._max_floor:
            raise InvalidFloorError(f"floor {floor} outside [{self._min_floor}, {self._max_floor}]")

    def _validate_hall_call(self, floor: int, direction: Direction) -> None:
        self._validate_floor(floor)

        if direction is Direction.IDLE:
            raise InvalidDirectionError("hall call direction cannot be IDLE")

        if direction is Direction.UP and floor == self._max_floor:
            raise InvalidDirectionError(f"cannot call UP from top floor {floor}")

        if direction is Direction.DOWN and floor == self._min_floor:
            raise InvalidDirectionError(f"cannot call DOWN from bottom floor {floor}")
```

🎙️ "I group the hall-call guards into `_validate_hall_call` so the public method stays short and reads top-to-bottom."

### Approach Comparison — `ElevatorCar.step()` (the most interesting method)

**Approach 1 — FIFO queue, serve in press order (rejected):**
```
pop the oldest pressed floor, move straight to it, open doors
```
> **Challenges:** the car yo-yos — press 2, 9, 3, 8 makes it run 2→9→3→8, doubling travel and starving nobody but wasting everyone's time. And a floor pressed twice enqueues twice → a phantom second stop.

**Approach 2 — recompute a full optimal route every tick (rejected for this scale):**
```
on every tick, solve "shortest tour covering all pending stops from current floor"
```
> **Challenges:** that's a traveling-salesman-flavored recompute every tick for zero benefit — on a 1-D line of floors SCAN is already optimal-ish, and the recompute is pure complexity with synchronisation risk. *I'd only revisit if cars could skip floors or had express zones.*

**Chosen approach — SCAN over a set (one direction until empty, then flip):**
```
if on a stop -> open; elif stops ahead -> step toward them; else flip and step
```
> **Why it wins:** single source of truth (the set), O(1) idempotent presses, no starvation (the far end is reached within one turnaround), and the "next floor" question is a direct set scan — no stored order to keep in sync.

### Verification — full-lifecycle dry runs

**Scenario 1 — Happy path (dispatch + SCAN up):**
Initial: car-A at 0, IDLE, `_stops={}`.

| Step | Call | After |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `request_elevator(3, UP)` → car-A | car-A `_stops={3}`, MOVING, UP |
| 2 | `select_floor("car-A", 8)` | `_stops={3,8}` |
| 3 | `select_floor("car-A", 5)` | `_stops={3,5,8}` |
| 4 | tick ×3 | car-A reaches 3, opens → `StopEvent(car-A,3,UP)`, `_stops={5,8}` |
| 5 | tick ×2 | reaches 5, opens → `StopEvent(car-A,5,UP)`, `_stops={8}` |
| 6 | tick ×3 | reaches 8, opens → `StopEvent(car-A,8,UP)`, `_stops={}` |
| 7 | tick | no stops → `_park()`, IDLE |

Served order: `[3, 5, 8]` ascending. ✓

**Scenario 2 — Idempotent double-press:**
Initial: car-A at 0, `_stops={}`.

| Step | Call | After |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `select_floor("car-A", 7)` | `_stops={7}` (request_stop → True) |
| 2 | `select_floor("car-A", 7)` | `_stops={7}` (request_stop → False, no change) |
| 3 | `select_floor("car-A", 7)` | `_stops={7}` (False again) |
| 4 | drain ticks | floor 7 emits exactly ONE `StopEvent` |

One stop, served once. ✓

**Scenario 3 — Race-prone scenario (plain English):** Without a lock, two passengers press buttons on an IDLE car-A at the same instant. Both threads enter `request_stop` and both see `state == IDLE`, so both head into `_orient_from_idle`, which `min`-s over `_stops` — i.e. **iterates** the set. While one thread iterates, the other's `self._stops.add(floor)` changes the set's size, and CPython raises `RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration` out of that thread. (The set itself never loses or duplicates a floor — add/in/discard are GIL-atomic — so the failure is specifically the unhandled iteration error, not a lost stop.) WITH the per-car lock, the second thread waits until the first finishes the whole add-and-orient, sees the car already MOVING, and just adds its floor — no iteration ever overlaps an add, so it is consistent every time. `test_lockfree_base_corrupts_under_same_car_race` demonstrates the throw against the lock-free base; the thread-safe test shows the same scenario clean.

### Complexity Summary

| Operation | Time | Space | Key reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `request_elevator` | O(c) | O(1) | scan c cars in dispatch (c = cars, tiny) + O(1) set add |
| `select_floor` | O(1) | O(1) | dict lookup + O(1) set add |
| `tick` | O(c · n) | O(c) | each of c cars does an O(n) "stop ahead?" scan (n = floors) |
| `set_maintenance` | O(s) | O(1) | clears the car's stop set (s = pending stops) |
| `pending_stops` | O(s) | O(s) | builds a frozenset snapshot |

## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

**Which flow and why:** I'm showing a hall call followed by ticks through to a door-open, because it touches every class — the orchestrator validates, the Strategy fires to pick a car, the car runs SCAN, and a `StopEvent` comes back.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor P as Passenger
    participant Sys as ElevatorControlSystem
    participant Strat as DispatchStrategy
    participant Car as ElevatorCar
    P->>Sys: request_elevator(3, UP)
    Sys->>Sys: validate floor and direction
    Sys->>Strat: select(in-service cars, 3, UP)
    Strat-->>Sys: car-A (nearest)
    Sys->>Car: request_stop(3) under per-car lock
    Car->>Car: add 3 to stop set then orient UP
    Car-->>Sys: True (newly added)
    Sys-->>P: car-A
    P->>Sys: tick()
    Sys->>Car: step() under per-car lock
    Car->>Car: move toward 3 then open doors at 3
    Car-->>Sys: StopEvent car-A floor 3 UP
    Sys-->>P: list with one StopEvent
```

> 🎙️ **Script — arrow by arrow:** "The passenger calls `request_elevator(3, UP)` on the system. The system validates the floor and direction itself, then delegates the 'which car' decision — it calls `select` on the strategy with only the in-service cars, and the strategy returns car-A. The interesting moment is `request_stop(3)` on the car, held inside the per-car lock — the car adds 3 to its set and orients itself UP; the orchestrator never touches the car's state. The car returns True (newly added), and the system hands the passenger car-A's id. Later a `tick()` calls `step()` on the car under the same lock — the car moves toward 3, opens its doors, and emits a `StopEvent`, which bubbles back as a one-element list."

**Why the sequence shows the design is good:** the orchestrator coordinates but implements no business logic — the 'which car' decision delegates to the Strategy and the 'how to move' decision delegates to the car's SCAN state machine. That's Open/Closed and Information Expert at work in one flow.

## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**DEFAULT: describe the solution; write code only if asked.**

🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race:** "The race is in `request_stop` when it orients an idle car. `_orient_from_idle` does `min(self._stops, ...)`, which **iterates** the stop set. If two passengers press buttons on the SAME car at the same instant, one thread's `self._stops.add(floor)` lands while the other is mid-iteration — and CPython raises `RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration`. I want to be precise: set membership (`add`/`in`/`discard`) is GIL-atomic, so no stop is ever lost or duplicated and the worst direction outcome is a benign last-writer-wins; the genuine, reproducible defect is the unhandled iteration error. It's a concurrent-mutation-during-iteration bug — the add and the orient's read have to be one atomic step."

🗣️ **Step 2 — The solution:** "I add a lock PER CAR. Before queueing or stepping a car, the orchestrator takes that car's lock; while held, no other thread can add to that car's set, so no add can land while `_orient_from_idle` is iterating. The add-and-orient becomes one atomic step."

🗣️ **Step 3 — The trade-off:** "Per-car locking, not one global lock, so ten different cars in the bank move fully in parallel — only presses on the SAME car serialise, and that's microseconds. The notify-style work (here, returning `StopEvent`s) happens outside the lock."

🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed case:** "Across multiple control servers an in-process lock isn't enough. I'd push atomicity to the shared store with a compare-and-set on the car's stop set — append the floor in a single conditional write — or route all calls for a given car to one owning server (consistent hashing on car id) so each car still has a single writer."

**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY**

| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
|---|---|---|
| **Race condition** | two threads touch shared state at once, result depends on timing | one thread's `_stops.add` lands while another iterates `_stops` in `_orient_from_idle` |
| **Critical section** | the block that must run as one atomic step | the add-and-orient inside `_lock(car)` |
| **Concurrent mutation during iteration** | mutate a collection while another thread iterates it → `RuntimeError` | `min(self._stops, ...)` racing `self._stops.add(floor)` |
| **Atomic** | all-or-nothing; no thread sees a half-done state | what the per-car lock guarantees |
| **Mutex / lock** | a token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` per car |
| **Reentrant (RLock)** | the SAME thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | a future guarded method calling another on the same car won't deadlock |
| **Lock granularity** | how much one lock covers — coarse vs fine | we chose per-CAR, not one global |
| **Pessimistic locking** | grab the lock before touching state | the in-process per-car RLock |
| **Optimistic locking** | don't lock; write conditionally, retry on loss | the distributed compare-and-set |
| **Compare-and-set (CAS)** | atomic "update only if value is still X" | the distributed conditional append |
| **Deadlock** | two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided — we hold only ONE car lock at a time |
| **Starvation** | a thread never gets the lock | not a risk — locks are held microseconds |

**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A**

> ❓ "Why per-car locks and not one global lock?"
> "A global lock serialises the whole bank — car-A in the lobby would block car-B in the penthouse even though they share nothing. Per-car locking lets every car move in parallel; only presses on the SAME car serialise, and that's a microsecond set operation. I also keep the `StopEvent` collection outside the lock so nothing slow runs while it's held."

> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
> "RLock is reentrant — the same thread can re-acquire it. Today no guarded method re-enters, but the moment a guarded method calls another guarded helper on the same car, a plain `Lock` would deadlock on the second acquire. RLock costs almost nothing and makes the design safe to extend."

> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
> "None here — a thread only ever holds ONE car lock at a time, and we never acquire a second lock while holding the first. No lock-ordering cycle means no deadlock. The map-lock in the thread-safe subclass is taken and released BEFORE the car lock, so it's not held across the critical section either."

> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is tiny and same-car contention is low, so a short-held lock beats a retry loop. For the DISTRIBUTED case I flip to optimistic — a compare-and-set conditional write on the shared store — because you can't hold a Python lock across servers."

> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-critical-section?"
> "In-process, a crash takes the whole process down, so there's no orphaned lock — a supervisor restarts from the persisted car state. In the distributed CAS version, the conditional write either fully committed or didn't, so there's no half-done state to clean up."

❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version**

**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass:**
```python copy
import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager


class ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem(ElevatorControlSystem):
    def __init__(self, cars, min_floor, max_floor, strategy=None):
        super().__init__(cars, min_floor, max_floor, strategy)
        # WHY: a guard around the dict of locks so two threads creating a new
        #      car's lock at once don't corrupt the dict.
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()
        # WHY: RLock (reentrant) so the same thread can re-enter safely.
        self._car_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, car) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: double-lock idiom — lock the map briefly to fetch-or-create the
        #      car's lock, then hold ONLY the per-car lock for the critical section.
        with self._map_lock:
            car_lock = self._car_locks.setdefault(car.car_id, threading.RLock())

        with car_lock:
            yield
```

**Part B — the EXACT modified method.** The only change from §6 to §9 is which `_lock` runs — the method body is byte-for-byte identical because the lock is a hook:
```python copy
# ─── §6 version (clean, single-threaded) — _lock(chosen) is a no-op ─────────
def request_elevator(self, floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str:
    self._validate_hall_call(floor, direction)
    candidates = [car for car in self._cars.values() if car.in_service()]
    chosen = self._strategy.select(candidates, floor, direction)
    if chosen is None:
        raise NoCarAvailableError(f"no in-service car for hall call at floor {floor}")
    with self._lock(chosen):          # ← no-op in §6
        chosen.request_stop(floor)
    return chosen.car_id

# ─── §9 version (thread-safe) — SAME body; _lock(chosen) now a real per-car RLock ─
# (no method rewrite needed — the subclass overrides _lock, so request_elevator,
#  select_floor, tick, and set_maintenance all become thread-safe unchanged)
```

🎙️ "The beauty of the hook is the diff is ZERO lines in the methods — `request_elevator`, `select_floor`, `tick`, and `set_maintenance` already call `self._lock(car)`. The subclass overrides only `_lock`, so all four become thread-safe with no edits. That's Template Method paying off."

**Part C — prove the lock is LOAD-BEARING with a paired barrier test** (in `tests/test_elevator.py`, run green above). The same scenario runs against BOTH implementations: the lock-free base must corrupt, the locked subclass must stay clean. A test that passed identically against both would prove nothing — so the proof is the *difference*.

```python copy
# Floors on BOTH sides of the start (25) so _orient_from_idle's `min` iterates a
# genuinely two-sided set; 40 threads press one duplicate floor, others press
# distinct floors. _run_barrier_scenario releases all of them via a Barrier and
# returns whatever errors any thread saw.

def test_lockfree_base_corrupts_under_same_car_race():
    # CONTROL: the lock-free base must raise "Set changed size during iteration"
    # — proving the bug is real and the GIL alone does NOT make it safe.
    sys.setswitchinterval(1e-6)                  # force aggressive thread switching
    saw = False
    for _ in range(200):                          # race is probabilistic; retry
        car = ElevatorCar(car_id="car-A", min_floor=0, max_floor=50, current_floor=25)
        errors = _run_barrier_scenario(ElevatorControlSystem([car], 0, 50))
        if any(isinstance(e, RuntimeError) and "changed size during iteration" in str(e) for e in errors):
            saw = True
            break
    assert saw, "lock-free base never threw -> the lock would not be load-bearing"

def test_threadsafe_clean_and_consistent_under_same_car_race():
    # Same scenario, same hostile switch interval, through the LOCKED subclass:
    # zero errors, no lost/duplicate stop, and a consistent committed direction.
    sys.setswitchinterval(1e-6)
    car = ElevatorCar(car_id="car-A", min_floor=0, max_floor=50, current_floor=25)
    system = ThreadSafeElevatorControlSystem([car], 0, 50)
    errors = _run_barrier_scenario(system)
    assert errors == []                           # NONE of the RuntimeError the base throws
    pending = system.pending_stops("car-A")
    assert pending == frozenset(DISTINCT_FLOORS) | {DUPLICATE_FLOOR}   # duplicate queued ONCE
    car_obj = system._require_car("car-A")
    assert car_obj.state is CarState.MOVING       # oriented toward a real pending stop
```
*(`sys.setswitchinterval` is saved and restored in a `finally` in the real tests; elided here for brevity.)*

🎙️ "The pairing is the point. The control test runs the EXACT barrier scenario against the lock-free base and asserts it throws `Set changed size during iteration` — so the bug is real and the GIL does not save us. The thread-safe test runs the SAME scenario, same switch interval, through the per-car lock and asserts it's clean with a consistent queue and direction. Earlier I made the mistake of pressing only floors ABOVE the start and asserting `direction is UP` — that passed even lock-free, because it was trivially true. Now stops straddle the start floor and I diff the two implementations, so the test actually proves the lock made the add-and-orient atomic."

**Edge cases handled**
- Floor out of range → `InvalidFloorError` → covered by `test_invalid_floor_rejected`
- UP-from-top / DOWN-from-bottom / IDLE hall call → `InvalidDirectionError` → `test_invalid_direction_rejected`
- Unknown car id → `UnknownCarError` → `test_unknown_car_rejected`
- Car call to out-of-service car → `CarOutOfServiceError` → `test_car_call_to_out_of_service_rejected`
- All cars out of service → `NoCarAvailableError` → `test_no_car_available_when_all_in_maintenance`
- Same floor pressed twice → one stop (silent) → `test_pressing_same_floor_twice_is_one_stop`
- Maintenance drops queued stops → `test_maintenance_drops_pending_stops`
- SCAN turnaround / no starvation → `test_scan_turnaround...`, `test_scan_no_starvation...`
- Concurrent same-car presses → lock-free base throws `Set changed size during iteration`, locked subclass stays clean → `test_lockfree_base_corrupts...` + `test_threadsafe_clean_and_consistent...`

**Extensibility — interviewer twists**

*(i) Level-calibration note:* the number of these tracks the target level — a junior gets none, mid-level one or two, senior several with depth. Here are three that prove the design bends without breaking.

*(ii) Summary table:*

| Want to add | How (one class/method) | What in the core changes |
|---|---|---|
| New dispatch policy (zone-based) | new `DispatchStrategy` subclass | nothing (inject it) |
| Express car (skips some floors) | new `CarState`/skip-set on `ElevatorCar` | one guard in `step` |
| Fire-recall / emergency mode | new `CarState.EMERGENCY` value | one branch in `step` |
| Priority/VIP hall call | richer arg to `select` | one line in the strategy |

*(iii) Twist walkthroughs:*

> **Twist 1 — "Add a least-busy / zone-based dispatch policy."**
> Currently dispatch is hardcoded to nearest-car only if you skipped the seam — but I built the Strategy seam exactly for this.
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "Zero core change. I write a new `DispatchStrategy` subclass — say `ZoneStrategy` that maps floor ranges to preferred cars — and inject it in the constructor. The orchestrator still just calls `select`."
> **The change:**
> ```python
> class ZoneStrategy(DispatchStrategy):
>     def __init__(self, zones): self._zones = zones  # car_id -> (lo, hi)
>     def select(self, cars, floor, direction):
>         in_zone = [c for c in cars if self._zones[c.car_id][0] <= floor <= self._zones[c.car_id][1]]
>         return NearestCarStrategy().select(in_zone or cars, floor, direction)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** zones improve rush-hour throughput but can leave a zone's cars idle while another zone queues — worth it in a tall mixed-use tower, overkill for a small building.

> **Twist 2 — "Cars need a fire-recall / emergency mode that sends them to the lobby."**
> Today a car has four states; emergency is a new one.
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I add an `EMERGENCY` value to `CarState`. Entering it clears the stop set, forces a single stop at the lobby, and blocks new calls — one new branch in `step` and a guard in `request_stop`."
> **The change:**
> ```python
> # CarState gains EMERGENCY
> def trigger_emergency(self, lobby: int) -> None:
>     self.state = CarState.EMERGENCY
>     self._stops = {lobby}
>     self.direction = Direction.UP if lobby >= self.current_floor else Direction.DOWN
> # request_stop adds: if self.state is CarState.EMERGENCY: raise CarOutOfServiceError(...)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** adds a state + one guard; mandatory for a real building's safety certification, scoped out of v1 here.

> **Twist 3 — "Support an express car that only stops at floors divisible by 10."**
> The car needs to ignore non-express floors.
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I give `ElevatorCar` a `stops_at(floor)` predicate (default: always True). `request_stop` rejects floors the car can't serve; the express car overrides the predicate. SCAN logic is untouched."
> **The change:**
> ```python
> def request_stop(self, floor):
>     if not self.stops_at(floor):           # express cars override this
>         raise InvalidFloorError(f"car {self.car_id} does not serve floor {floor}")
>     ...  # rest identical
> ```
> **Trade-off:** the dispatcher must route non-express requests to a non-express car — a strategy concern, not a core one.

**Hardest follow-up questions**

> ❓ "What's the hardest concurrency case in THIS design?"
> "Two passengers pressing buttons on the SAME idle car at the same instant. `_orient_from_idle` iterates the stop set via `min`, and a concurrent `set.add` mutates it mid-iteration — `RuntimeError: Set changed size during iteration`. The per-car RLock makes the add-and-orient atomic so no add lands during the iteration. I prove it both ways: a control test shows the lock-free base actually throws that error under a barrier, and the thread-safe test runs the same scenario clean with a consistent queue and direction. Different cars never contend because each has its own lock."

> ❓ "What if you have multiple control servers (distributed)?"
> "An in-process lock only protects one process. I'd make each car's state authoritative in a shared store and either (a) route all calls for a car to one owning server via consistent hashing on car id — so each car has a single writer — or (b) use an optimistic compare-and-set when appending a stop. Both keep the per-car atomicity guarantee across servers."

> ❓ "What if you have 10M buildings / a huge bank?"
> "Dispatch is O(cars-in-this-bank), which is tiny — banks have a handful of cars. Scale is horizontal across buildings: each building is an independent shard with its own `ElevatorControlSystem`, partitioned by building id. Nothing in the core couples one building to another, so it scales linearly."

> ❓ "What if a `tick` fails halfway — a car's motor faults mid-move?"
> "Each car steps independently under its own lock, so one car faulting doesn't corrupt others. I'd catch the fault, put that car into MAINTENANCE (which drops its queue so passengers re-request on another car via dispatch), and continue ticking the rest. The compensation is built in — a faulted car simply leaves the dispatch pool."

> 🎙️ **Final 30-second summary:** "The key insight is naming the algorithm — SCAN — and making each car a self-guarding state machine that owns its stops as a set, so a double-press is one stop by construction and no floor starves. The orchestrator coordinates through one Strategy seam — `DispatchStrategy`, with nearest-car and least-busy concretes — so new dispatch rules are new classes, the core never changes. The one concurrency risk is that `_orient_from_idle` iterates the stop set while a concurrent press adds to it — `Set changed size during iteration`; I guard it with a per-car RLock via a Template-Method hook, proved by a PAIRED barrier test (lock-free base throws, locked subclass clean). For distributed scale, I'd push the per-car atomicity to a compare-and-set or route each car to a single owning server."
